name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ ۖ قُلْ مَآ أَنفَقْتُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ فَلِلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ ۗ وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ (٢١٥)
```

```english
They ask you as to what they should spend. Say, ‘Let whatever wealth you spend be for the parents, relatives, orphans, the needy, and the traveller.’ Allah indeed knows whatever good you do. (215)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ ۖ قُلْ مَا أَنفَقْتُم مِّنْ خَيْرٍ** — "They ask you as to what they should spend. Say, ‘Whatever good you spend...’" (2:215). They [the grammarians and rhetoricians] have said: This verse is structured according to the "style of wisdom" (أسلوب الحكمة). For they only asked about the substance and type of what they should spend, yet this question was almost redundant because it is obvious what is used for spending—namely, wealth (المال) in its various forms. What was more deserving of the question was: to whom should one spend? Thus, the response was diverted to address the recipients and clarify their types, as a reminder to them of what the proper question ought to have been.

What they mentioned is an eloquent perspective; however, they overlooked something. The verse, despite this, does address the substance of what they spend. It addresses this: first, by its saying, "of good" (من خير), in a general sense; and second, by its saying, "And whatever good you do, Allah indeed knows it," (وما تفعلوا من خير فإن الله به عليم). Thus, in the verse is an indication that what is spent is wealth, whatever it may be, whether little or much, and that doing so is a righteous deed (فعل خير) of which Allah is fully aware. However, it was incumbent upon them to ask about those for whom they spend and to know them, and they are: the parents, the kinsmen, the orphans, the needy, and the traveller.

Among the strange opinions is what some commentators have mentioned: that the meaning of "what" (ما) in His saying, the Exalted, "what they should spend" (ماذا ينفقون), is not a question about the quiddity (الماهية) — *the essential nature of a thing that makes it what it is* — for that is a logical convention (اصطلاح منطقي) that should not be imposed upon Arabic speech, especially the most eloquent and articulate of speech. Rather, they claim it is a question about the modality (الكيفية) — *the manner or way in which something is done* — and how they should spend it and in what place they should put it. Thus, the answer was given by directing it to those mentioned in the verse, making the answer correspond to the question, contrary to what the scholars of rhetoric have stated!

Similar to this, and even stranger, is what another group mentioned: that although the question uses the word "what" (ما), the intent is to ask about the modality. For it is well known that what is spent is wealth; since this is known, the mind would not turn toward it, and it becomes certain that the question is about the modality. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **قَالُوا ادْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ يُبَيِّن لَّنَا مَا هِيَ إِنَّ الْبَقَرَ تَشَابَهَ عَلَيْنَا** — "They said, ‘Invoke your Lord for us that He may clarify for us what she is; indeed the cows are much alike to us’" (2:70). It was already known that a cow is a beast of such-and-such nature and description, so there is no reason to interpret His saying, "what she is" (ما هي), as a request for the quiddity (الماهية). Thus, it was certain that it was a question about the attribute (الصفة) by which this cow is distinguished from others. Therefore, the answer was given in correspondence by His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا ذَلُولٌ** — "He says, ‘She is a cow not broken...’" (2:71).

The matter has become confused for these people. For even if "what" (ما) was not linguistically coined to request the quiddity (الماهية) as defined in the convention of logic (المنطق)—which is the definition (الحد) composed of the proximate genus (الجنس) and differentia (الفصل)—this does not necessitate that it was then coined to ask about the modality (الكيفية). It would not be correct for someone to say, when asking about those entitled to spending: "What (ماذا) should I spend?" meaning "Upon whom (على من) should I spend?" such that the answer would be: "For the parents and kinsmen." That would be the clearest form of linguistic error (اللحن).

Rather, "what" (ما) is coined to ask about that by which a thing is known, whether it is known by its definition (الحد) and quiddity (الماهية), or known by its properties (الخواص) and attributes (الأوصاف). Thus, it is broader than the logical convention, not distinct from it or specifically coined to ask about the modality (الكيفية) of a thing. From this, it is known that His saying, the Exalted, "clarify for us what she is" (يبين لنا ما هي), and His saying, "She is a cow not broken" (إنها بقرة لا ذلول), are a question and answer proceeding according to the linguistic root, which is asking about that which identifies and specifies a thing, and answering accordingly.

As for the claim that since the quiddity (الماهية) was known, it became necessary to interpret "what" (ما) as a question about the modality rather than the quiddity—this is a most obvious error. For the fact that something is known does not necessitate changing the meaning of a word from what it was coined for to something else.

Following these two in strangeness is the opinion of those who say: the question was about both matters together: "What (ما) should they spend?" and "Where (أين) should they spend?" Thus, one of the two questions was mentioned and the other was omitted—namely the second question—because the answer points to it! This is as you can see [in its weakness].

In any case, there should be no doubt that in the verse there is a certain diversion of the answer to another answer, as a reminder (تنبيهاً) that what is more deserving is to ask about those who are spent upon. Otherwise, the fact that spending is from "good" (الخير) and wealth is obvious. The diversion from one meaning to another to draw attention to what one ought to turn toward and occupy oneself with occurs frequently in the Quran. It is among the most subtle of literary devices (الصنائع) unique to it, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي يَنْعِقُ بِمَا لَا يَسْمَعُ إِلَّا دُعَاءً وَنِدَاءً** — "The parable of the faithless is that of someone who shouts after that which hears nothing except a call and cry" (2:171); and His saying, the Exalted: **مَثَلُ مَا يُنفِقُونَ فِي هَٰذِهِ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا كَمَثَلِ رِيحٍ فِيهَا صِرٌّ** — "The parable of what they spend in the life of this world is that of a freezing wind..." (3:117); and His saying, the Exalted: **مَّثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ أَمْوَالَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنَابِلَ** — "The parable of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is that of a grain which sends up seven ears..." (2:261); and His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ لَا يَنفَعُ مَالٌ وَلَا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ** — "The day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart" (26:88-89); and His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ مَا أَسْأَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَن شَاءَ أَن يَتَّخِذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا** — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, except that anyone who wishes should take the way to his Lord’" (24:57); and His saying, the Exalted: **سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ إِلَّا عِبَادَ اللَّهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ** — "Clear is Allah of what they allege, [all] except Allah’s chosen servants" (37:159-160), among other noble verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا تَفْعَلُوا مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ بِهِ عَلِيمٌ** — "And whatever good you do, Allah indeed knows it." The substitution of "spending" (الإنفاق) with "doing good" (فعل الخير) here is like the substitution of "wealth" (المال) with "good" (الخير) at the beginning of the verse. It is an allusion (إيماء) to the fact that although spending is recommended (مندوباً إليه) from wealth, whether little or much, it ought to be "good" (خيراً) that attracts desire and upon which love falls, as He, the Exalted, said: **لَن تَنَالُوا الْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنفِقُوا مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ** — "You will never attain piety until you spend out of what you love" (3:92); and as He, the Exalted, said: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنفِقُوا مِن طَيِّبَاتِ مَا كَسَبْتُمْ وَمِمَّا أَخْرَجْنَا لَكُم مِّنَ الْأَرْضِ ۖ وَلَا تَيَمَّمُوا الْخَبِيثَ مِنْهُ تُنفِقُونَ وَلَسْتُم بِآخِذِيهِ إِلَّا أَن تُغْمِضُوا فِيهِ** — "O you who have faith! Spend of the good things you have earned [honestly] and of what We bring forth for you from the earth, and do not intend to give the bad part of it, for you would not take it yourselves except with closed eyes" (2:267).

It is also an allusion that spending should not be in the manner of evil, such as spending accompanied by reminders of generosity (المن) and annoyance (الأذى), as He, the Exalted, said: **ثُمَّ لَا يُتْبِعُونَ مَا أَنفَقُوا مَنًّا وَلَا أَذًى** — "...and then do not follow up what they have spent with reproaches or affronts" (2:262); and His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَسْأَلُونَكَ مَاذَا يُنفِقُونَ قُلِ الْعَفْوَ** — "And they ask you as to what they should spend. Say, ‘The surplus’" (2:219).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "I have not seen a people better than the companions of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم; they did not ask him except about thirteen matters until he passed away, all of which are in the Quran. Among them are: 'They ask you about wine and gambling,' 'They ask you about the sacred month,' 'They ask you about the orphans,' 'They ask you about menstruation,' 'They ask you about the spoils of war,' and 'They ask you what they should spend.' They did not ask except about what would benefit them."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding the verse: It was revealed concerning Amr ibn al-Jamuh, who was an elderly man with much wealth. He said: "O Messenger of Allah! With what should I give charity? And upon whom should I give charity?" So Allah revealed this verse.

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn al-Mundhir from Ibn Hayyan, but they have considered the narration weak. Furthermore, it does not correspond to the verse, as the verse only mentions the question about what is given in charity, not those upon whom it is given.

Similar to it in lack of correspondence is what was also narrated from Ibn Jarir and Ibn al-Mundhir from Ibn Jurayj, who said: "The believers asked the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم where they should place their wealth, so 'They ask you what they should spend. Say, whatever good you spend...' was revealed. That refers to voluntary spending (النفقة في التطوع), and the zakat (الزكاة) is something else entirely."

Also similar to it is what was narrated from al-Suddi, who said: "On the day this verse was revealed, there was no zakat; it refers to the spending a man does on his family and the charity he gives, then the zakat abrogated (نسختها) it."

**I say:** The relationship between the verse of zakat: **خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوَالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً** — "Take charity from their possessions" (9:103), and this verse is not one of abrogation (النسخ) — *the replacement of a previous legal ruling with a new one* — as is evident, unless he intended by "abrogation" another meaning.
